steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a sentence boundary question. The blank sits between "this legendary lost work" and "That fantasy (partially) became reality in 2022." The phrase "That fantasy…" is an independent clause with its own subject and verb, so the preceding clause must be separated from it by punctuation strong enough to end a sentence.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'work, that': A comma alone between two independent clauses creates a comma splice, which violates Standard English boundaries. ✗
  • Choice B — 'work and that': The coordinating conjunction "and" can join two independent clauses only when preceded by a comma ("work, and that"). Without the comma this is a run-on. ✗
  • Choice C — 'work that': No punctuation between two independent clauses is a fused (run-on) sentence, the most severe boundary error. ✗
  • Choice D — 'work. That': A period fully ends the first independent clause and lets "That fantasy…" open a new sentence correctly. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
